source_paper_title: Improved Online Contention Resolution for Matchings and Applications to the Gig Economy
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Pollner_2024, title={Improved Online Contention Resolution for Matchings and Applications to the Gig Economy}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1388}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1388}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Pollner, Tristan and Roghani, Mohammad and Saberi, Amin and Wajc, David}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1582–1606} }
context:
Section 5 (Discussion), page 20

Our algorithm, as well as all prior work on (RO-)OCRS for matching constraints, works with the degree-bounded relaxation for matchings, which is integral for bipartite graphs, but not for general ones. A natural question is whether better (RO-)OCRS can be achieved for points in the matching polytope, i.e., points which also satisfy Edmonds’ odd-set constraints [Edm65]. Can better bounds be achieved for this polytope, perhaps via a different algorithmic approach?
title: Improve contention resolution for points in the exact matching polytope setting
background_latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an (undirected) graph. A (fractional) vector \(x\in[0,1]^E\) assigns a value \(x_e\) to each edge \(e\in E\). The (degree-bounded) matching relaxation is
\[P(G):=\Bigl\{x\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^E: \sum_{e\ni v} x_e\le 1\ \forall v\in V\Bigr\}.
\]
The (integral) matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^E\) is the convex hull of incidence vectors of matchings in \(G\). Edmonds' characterization gives
\[\mathrm{MP}(G)=\Bigl\{x\in P(G): \sum_{e\subseteq S} x_e \le \frac{|S|-1}{2}\ \forall S\subseteq V\ \text{with }|S|\text{ odd}\Bigr\},
\]
where \(\sum_{e\subseteq S}\) denotes the sum over edges with both endpoints in \(S\) (the odd-set constraints).

A random-order online contention resolution scheme (RO-OCRS) for matchings takes as input a fractional point \(x\) (typically in some polytope such as \(P(G)\) or \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\)). Then edges arrive online in a uniformly random order. Each edge \(e\) is independently declared active with probability \(x_e\). Upon seeing an active edge \(e\), the scheme must irrevocably accept it into the output set \(I\) or reject it, while maintaining that \(I\) is a matching.

A RO-OCRS is called \(c\)-balanced if for every graph \(G\), every feasible input point \(x\), and every edge \(e\in E\), the output matching \(I\) satisfies
\[\Pr[e\in I]\ge c\,x_e.
\]
Here probability is over the scheme’s internal randomness, the random arrival order, and the independent activations.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a constant \(c>0.456\) and a (polynomial-time) RO-OCRS for matchings that is \(c\)-balanced when the input fractional point \(x\) is restricted to lie in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) (i.e., satisfies both degree constraints and Edmonds' odd-set constraints). More generally, determine the best achievable balance factor
\[c^*:=\sup\{c: \exists\ \text{a (polytime) RO-OCRS that is }c\text{-balanced for all }G\text{ and all }x\in \mathrm{MP}(G)\}.
\]
latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an (undirected) graph. A (fractional) vector \(x\in[0,1]^E\) assigns a value \(x_e\) to each edge \(e\in E\). The (degree-bounded) matching relaxation is
\[P(G):=\Bigl\{x\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^E: \sum_{e\ni v} x_e\le 1\ \forall v\in V\Bigr\}.
\]

\[\mathrm{MP}(G)=\Bigl\{x\in P(G): \sum_{e\subseteq S} x_e \le \frac{|S|-1}{2}\ \forall S\subseteq V\ \text{with }|S|\text{ odd}\Bigr\},
\]
where \(\sum_{e\subseteq S}\) denotes the sum over edges with both endpoints in \(S\) (the odd-set constraints).

A random-order online contention resolution scheme (RO-OCRS) for matchings takes as input a fractional point \(x\) (typically in some polytope such as \(P(G)\) or \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\)). Then edges arrive online in a uniformly random order. Each edge \(e\) is independently declared active with probability \(x_e\). Upon seeing an active edge \(e\), the scheme must irrevocably accept it into the output set \(I\) or reject it, while maintaining that \(I\) is a matching.

A RO-OCRS is called \(c\)-balanced if for every graph \(G\), every feasible input point \(x\), and every edge \(e\in E\), the output matching \(I\) satisfies

\]

Determine whether there exists a constant \(c>0.456\) and a (polynomial-time) RO-OCRS for matchings that is \(c\)-balanced when the input fractional point \(x\) is restricted to lie in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) (i.e., satisfies both degree constraints and Edmonds' odd-set constraints). \]
open_problem_quote: Our algorithm, as well as all prior work on (RO-)OCRS for matching constraints, works with the degree-bounded relaxation for matchings, which is integral for bipartite graphs, but not for general ones. A natural question is whether better (RO-)OCRS can be achieved for points in the matching polytope, i.e., points which also satisfy Edmonds’ odd-set constraints [Edm65]. Can better bounds be achieved for this polytope, perhaps via a different algorithmic approach?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search for small graphs and fractional points in the matching polytope that appear hard for candidate RO-OCRS policies, but the space of online policies is large and proving optimal lower bounds is difficult. Still, testing specific algorithms and generating adversarial instances is feasible via simulation and optimization over small graphs.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate RO-OCRS policies (e.g., parametric attenuation/priority rules) can be represented as code with tunable parameters, and their worst-case (or empirically minimized) balancedness can be estimated by Monte Carlo over random orders and by adversarial search over small graphs and fractional inputs. This provides an automatic fitness signal (estimated minimum \(\Pr[e\in I]/x_e\)) enabling iterative improvement or discovery of counterexamples to proposed designs.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Pollner–Roghani–Saberi–Wajc (2022), the state of the art for random-order online contention resolution for matchings under independent edge activations is a \(0.45\)-balanced RO-OCRS for general graphs and \(0.456\)-balanced for bipartite graphs, analyzed for inputs \(x\in P(G)\) (degree constraints only). The paper explicitly raises the stronger question of whether restricting inputs to the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\)—i.e., additionally enforcing Edmonds’ odd-set constraints—permits a better balance factor, asking in particular whether one can beat \(0.456\) in this more structured setting.

The forward-citing literature summarized here does not resolve that question. The most directly relevant progress is indirect: “Towards an Optimal Contention” improves constants for offline CRSs for matchings (exceeding \(0.5\) in bipartite graphs and approaching the Karp–Sipser barrier \(\approx 0.544\) in a sparse regime for general graphs), but it neither operates online in random order nor leverages odd-set constraints. “Stationary Online Contention Resolution Schemes” develops a principled framework for permutation-invariant (stationary) OCRSs and proves constants \(1/3\) (general graphs) and \(\approx 0.382\) (bipartite) within that restricted class, suggesting that any improvement beyond \(0.456\) will require non-stationary behavior and/or new uses of polyhedral structure.

Several related-tool papers (on online dependent rounding, SOCS, and probe-commit/configuration-LP approaches) demonstrate that stronger per-edge guarantees are achievable in other online matching models by combining richer rounding primitives (negative dependence, type decompositions, configuration LPs) with local contention resolution. However, none of these works provides a random-order edge-arrival OCRS with \(\Pr[e\in I]\ge c x_e\) for all \(x\in\mathrm{MP}(G)\), nor do they quantify how odd-set constraints might be exploited to surpass the \(0.456\) barrier. Thus, based on the provided forward citations, the problem remains open; a promising direction is to design RO-OCRSs whose acceptance/attenuation decisions depend on odd-set structure (e.g., via laminar odd-cut decompositions or blossom-based online certificates) rather than only local degree slack, potentially bridging the gap between online constants (\(\approx 0.456\)) and offline benchmarks (\(>0.5\)).

sources.title: Towards an Optimal Contention
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives improved contention resolution schemes (CRSs) for matchings under independent edge sampling from a fractional matching. In particular:
- For bipartite graphs and any fractional matching satisfying only vertex constraints \(\sum_{e\ni v} x_e\le 1\), it constructs an offline (non-monotone) CRS with \(\Pr[e\in M\mid e\in R(x)]\ge 0.509\) for all edges \(e\) (Theorem 2 / Theorem 5).
- For general graphs, assuming \(\|x\|_\infty\le \varepsilon\) and \(\sum_{e\ni v}x_e=1\) for all \(v\), it gives a CRS with balance \(\gamma-f(\varepsilon)\) where \(\gamma=2(1-\lambda)-\lambda^2\approx 0.544\) and \(\lambda=e^{-\lambda}\) (Theorem 1 / Theorem 3).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a random-order online OCRS with balance \(c>0.456\) when the input \(x\) lies in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) (i.e., includes odd-set constraints), and more generally the best achievable \(c^*\) in that setting.

This paper is primarily about offline CRSs (not online, not random-order online), and it does not exploit the odd-set constraints of \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\); in fact it states its results assuming only the degree constraints \(\sum_{e\ni v}x_e\le 1\), which is a superset of \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\). Therefore it does not resolve the online question nor determine \(c^*\) for RO-OCRS on \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\).

However, it is strongly relevant as partial progress/upper-envelope context: it shows that offline balancedness can exceed 0.5 (0.509 for bipartite) and can approach the Karp–Sipser barrier \(\approx 0.544\) for general graphs under \(\|x\|_\infty\to 0\). These results sharpen the landscape in which the RO-OCRS-for-\(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) problem sits, and they suggest that any online scheme is inherently weaker (they emphasize separations between offline and random-order online factors for bipartite matchings).

sources.title: Online dependent rounding schemes for bipartite matchings, with applications
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces online dependent rounding schemes (ODRSes) for online bipartite matchings/b-matchings with per-edge guarantee \(\Pr[e\in M]\ge \alpha x_e\). Main theorem (Theorem 1.2 / 3.9–3.10): there exists a (polytime, up to \(\gamma\)) bipartite matching ODRS with rounding ratio \(\alpha\approx 0.652\) (and \(0.646\) for b-matchings), breaking the \(1-1/e\) barrier for unrestricted fractional matchings \(x\in P(G)\) in the one-sided online-vertex-arrival model.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a random-order online contention resolution scheme (RO-OCRS) for general (not necessarily bipartite) matchings with balance factor \(c>0.456\) when the input \(x\) lies in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) (i.e., includes odd-set constraints). This paper studies a different online rounding model (ODRS) for bipartite graphs under adversarial one-sided vertex arrivals, and it targets unrestricted fractional matchings \(x\in P(G)\) (degree constraints only). It achieves a much larger constant (0.652) but does not address random-order edge arrivals, does not handle general graphs/nonbipartite matchings, and does not exploit/require \(x\in \mathrm{MP}(G)\) with odd-set constraints. Thus it does not solve the stated RO-OCRS-for-\(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) problem, but it provides partial progress in the broader landscape of online rounding/CRS for matchings by introducing new techniques (grouping + offline CRS, negative dependence) that might inspire improved RO-OCRS analyses.

sources.title: Stationary Online Contention Resolution Schemes
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces stationary OCRSs (S-OCRSs) and gives explicit selectability guarantees for several feasibility systems. For matchings in general graphs (i.e., the degree-bounded relaxation), it proves existence of a \(1/3\)-selectable S-OCRS (Theorem 1). For bipartite matchings (where \(P(G)=\mathrm{MP}(G)\)), it proves an \(\alpha^*\)-selectable S-OCRS with \(\alpha^*=(3-\sqrt5)/2\approx0.382\), and shows this is optimal within the stationary class (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether one can beat \(0.456\) for random-order OCRSs when the input \(x\) lies in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) (non-bipartite, with odd-set constraints). This paper does not analyze the \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) setting for general graphs; its matching results for non-bipartite graphs assume only the degree constraints \(x\in P(G)\) and achieve \(1/3\), far below \(0.456\). It does obtain \(\approx0.382\) for bipartite matchings, but bipartite graphs already satisfy \(\mathrm{MP}(G)=P(G)\) and the constant is still below \(0.456\). Thus it does not resolve the question of improving beyond \(0.456\) for \(x\in\mathrm{MP}(G)\) in general graphs; it provides related techniques (stationary/maximum-entropy/Gibbs) and partial progress in special environments.

sources.title: Prophet matching in the probe-commit model
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives online algorithms for bipartite stochastic matching in the probe-commit model with downward-closed probing constraints, achieving competitive ratios against the offline adaptive benchmark: (i) 
\[\mathbb{E}[w(M(\pi))] \ge \tfrac12\,\mathrm{OPT}(H_{\mathrm{typ}},(D_i)_{i=1}^n)\]
for adversarial arrival order \(\pi\) (Theorem 1), and (ii)
\[\mathbb{E}[w(M)] \ge (1-1/e)\,\mathrm{OPT}(H_{\mathrm{typ}},(D_i)_{i=1}^n)\]
for uniformly random arrival order (Theorem 3). The analysis uses a configuration LP (LP-config / LP-config-id) and applies rank-1 matroid online/random-order contention resolution schemes to resolve conflicts at each offline vertex.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a random-order online contention resolution scheme (RO-OCRS) for matchings that is \(c\)-balanced for all graphs when the fractional input \(x\) lies in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\), and in particular whether one can beat \(0.456\).

This paper does not study OCRS for the matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) nor does it give a \(c\)-balanced RO-OCRS guarantee of the form \(\Pr[e\in I]\ge c x_e\) for arbitrary fractional \(x\in\mathrm{MP}(G)\). Instead it studies stochastic probing/prophet matching with commitment, where the algorithm first solves a different LP (a configuration LP capturing probing strategies) and then uses contention resolution only as a subroutine to choose among multiple edges that “commit” to the same offline vertex. The contention resolution used is essentially rank-1 matroid RCRS/OCRS (yielding factors \(1-1/e\) in random order and \(1/2\) in adversarial order) applied per offline vertex, not a global OCRS for matchings with input \(x\in\mathrm{MP}(G)\).

Thus the paper is at most indirectly relevant: it leverages random-order contention resolution ideas and achieves a \(1-1/e\) factor in a different stochastic matching model, but it does not improve the balance factor for RO-OCRS on \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) beyond \(0.456\) or address that question.

sources.title: Stochastic online correlated selection
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces Stochastic Online Correlated Selection (SOCS) and a type-decomposition reduction from general fractional allocations to one-way/two-way surrogate types, and designs improved two-way SOCS/OCS algorithms for several online matching/allocation problems. For unweighted/vertex-weighted online stochastic matching it proves an optimal two-way SOCS convergence rate \(g(y)=(1+y)e^{-2y}\) (Theorem 5) and derives improved competitive ratios (e.g. 0.69 non-IID, 0.705 random-order). It does not study random-order online contention resolution schemes for edges with independent activations \(x_e\) in general graphs nor balance factors for inputs \(x\in \mathrm{MP}(G)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a random-order online contention resolution scheme (RO-OCRS) for matchings in an undirected graph where edges arrive in random order and are independently activated with probabilities \(x_e\), with \(x\) restricted to the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\), and seeks a balance factor \(c>0.456\). This paper instead considers SOCS/OCS frameworks for stochastic online matching/AdWords/Display Ads with vertex arrivals and fractional allocations \(\mu^t\), using type decomposition to reduce to two-way instances and proving convergence-rate bounds. While conceptually related (online rounding with negative correlation), it does not address RO-OCRS for edge arrivals, does not exploit Edmonds’ odd-set constraints, and does not provide a \(c\)-balanced OCRS guarantee of the form \(\Pr[e\in I]\ge c x_e\) for all edges under \(x\in \mathrm{MP}(G)\). Thus it offers at most indirect techniques (negative correlation, two-way reductions) rather than progress on the stated \(c^*\) question.

sources.title: Approximation Algorithms for Action-Reward Query-Commit Matching
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies action-reward query-commit matching with patience constraints on bipartite graphs. Its main result (Theorem 1.1) gives polynomial-time policies achieving approximation ratios
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a random-order online contention resolution scheme (RO-OCRS) for matchings with balance factor exceeding 0.456 when the fractional input lies in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) (i.e., includes odd-set constraints). This paper does not study OCRS for \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) or use odd-set constraints; instead it studies stochastic/query-commit matching with patience constraints and develops a different rounding framework (configuration LP + a patience random-order contention resolution subroutine on star graphs) to approximate an optimal adaptive policy. While it uses the language of random-order contention resolution and provides selection guarantees (e.g., \(1-1/e\) or \(\approx 0.5801\)) for its P-RCRS subproblem, these guarantees apply to a different model (query-commit with success probabilities and patience) and do not translate directly into a \(c\)-balanced RO-OCRS for \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\). Thus it offers potentially relevant techniques (random-order contention resolution analysis, attenuation, configuration LP rounding) but does not resolve the stated OCRS balance-factor question for \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\).

sources.title: Limitations of stochastic selection problems with pairwise independent priors
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves tight bounds for contention resolution and prophet inequalities on matroids under pairwise-independent (not fully independent) stochastic inputs. In particular:
- (Theorem 5.4) For any matroid of rank \(r\), there exists a \(\tfrac{1}{4r}\)-balanced online contention resolution scheme against the almighty adversary for any pairwise-independent active-set distribution with marginals in the matroid polytope.
- (Theorem 5.1) For the full linear matroid \(\mathbb{F}_q^d\times[d]\), no pairwise-independent offline CRS can be \((c+1)/d\)-balanced when \(q^{c-1}\ge d\); in particular no \(3/d\)-balanced scheme for \(q\ge d\).
- (Theorem 6.2) No \(\omega(1/\log r)\)-competitive prophet inequality exists for pairwise-independent priors on certain binary matroids (even vs oblivious adversary), while (Theorem 6.9) an \(\Omega(1/\log r)\)-competitive algorithm exists for all matroids vs almighty adversary.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for improving the balance factor of random-order online contention resolution schemes for matchings when the fractional point \(x\) lies in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) (i.e., under independent activations with probabilities \(x_e\) and random arrival order). This paper does not study matchings under independent Bernoulli activations; instead it studies matroids under pairwise-independent (correlated) priors, and its positive/negative results are about how much balance/competitiveness is possible under that weaker independence assumption.

Thus it neither provides a RO-OCRS for matchings with \(c>0.456\) on \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) nor addresses the effect of adding Edmonds' odd-set constraints. At most, it is tangentially relevant as it develops CRS/OCRS techniques and impossibility constructions in a different stochastic model, which could inform proof techniques but does not advance the specific constant \(0.456\) barrier for matchings in the independent-activation, random-order model.

sources.title: Online bipartite matching in the probe-commit model
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies online bipartite matching in the probe-commit model and proves optimal competitive ratios against an offline adaptive probing benchmark in several settings. In particular, for known independently distributed (i.d.) instances with random-order arrivals, it gives a polynomial-time algorithm with competitive ratio \(1-1/e\) (Corollary 5.6), using a new configuration LP (LP-config) and a reduction to a \((1-1/e)\)-selectable random-order contention resolution scheme (RCRS) for a 1-uniform matroid. It also proves a \(1/e\) ratio for edge-weighted worst-case instances with random-order arrivals (Theorem 6.3) and \((1-1/e)\) for certain vertex-weighted worst-case instances under rankability (Theorem 7.7).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for improving the balance factor \(c\) of a random-order online contention resolution scheme (RO-OCRS) for matchings when the fractional input \(x\) lies in the exact matching polytope \(\mathrm{MP}(G)\), i.e., exploiting odd-set constraints to beat \(0.456\). This paper does not study RO-OCRS for the matching polytope nor contention resolution for general graphs under \(x\in\mathrm{MP}(G)\). Instead, it uses contention resolution only as a subroutine for a different stochastic/probing matching model, and specifically reduces to contention resolution for a 1-uniform matroid on each offline vertex’s incident proposals (a per-vertex capacity-1 constraint), not to the matching polytope constraints (including odd-set constraints). Thus it neither resolves nor directly advances the stated OCRS-for-\(\mathrm{MP}(G)\) question, though it provides related LP/configuration and CRS-based techniques in online matching contexts.
